forecasts.reasoning: John Morgan has a history of teasing runs for free publicity without ultimately running (as in 2018). While he is establishing a new centrist party and there's a deadline of June 12, 2025, to affiliate with that party to run under its banner in 2026, simply changing his party registration does not require a formal campaign announcement. More importantly, Morgan explicitly stated in May 2025 that he could wait longer than Jason Pizzo (whose announcement is expected in September 2025) to open a campaign account due to his high name recognition. Therefore, an announcement before the short deadline of July 1, 2025 is highly unlikely, as he plans to wait and see how the field shapes up later in the year.

With only 24 days remaining between the knowledge cutoff (June 6, 2025) and resolution date (July 1, 2025), the probability of an official cancellation announcement is low. While Bulgargaz has ceased payments since July 2024 and is effectively bankrupt, and political leaders (Borissov) are actively pushing for an exit or amendment (May 2025), a unilateral cancellation by Bulgaria would trigger a massive BGN 3–4 billion penalty due to the 'take-or-pay' clause. Consequently, they are prioritizing renegotiation, and BOTAŞ has offered counter-proposals. Though Bulgargaz has hired a Swiss law firm to evaluate termination options, international contract disputes and negotiations typically drag on. BOTAŞ is unlikely to hastily terminate while high-level political talks and commercial renegotiations are ongoing, and Bulgaria is constrained by the financial ruin a unilateral termination would cause. Given the extremely narrow timeframe, an explicit cancellation before July 1 is unlikely.

Sudan has fundamentally failed to ratify the AfCFTA as of February 2025, and there is an ongoing, devastating civil war that effectively suspends all regular legislative and non-essential executive governmental functions. The ongoing conflict between the Sudanese Armed Forces and Rapid Support Forces makes issues like international trade agreements negligible compared to regime survival and humanitarian concerns. The lack of a functioning, recognized parliament or stable transitional government eliminates the standard domestic political apparatus necessary for ratification. With the deadline of July 1, 2025 rapidly approaching, there is virtually no capacity and no political will to finalize and ratify this complicated trade framework. The chance of it happening by July 1 is negligible.
